revision.comment: Added short description.
revision.text:
{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)
{{jmyth navbox long}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nihon Ryoiki}} [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Late Old Japanese texts]] [[Category:Heian period in literature]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese books]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist texts]] [[Category:Books about Buddhism in the Heian period]]

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)
{{jmyth navbox long}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nihon Ryoiki}} [[Category:Setsuwa]] [[Category:Late Old Japanese texts]] [[Category:Heian period in literature]] [[Category:9th-century Japanese books]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist texts]] [[Category:Books about Buddhism in the Heian period]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]]

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==

==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

revision.text:
{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.

While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}

All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.

While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[:en:Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1606838]] Wikidata name: Nihon Ryōiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[:en:Japan|Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[:en:Translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[:en:Epilogue|Epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[:en:Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[:en:Japan|Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[:en:Translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[:en:Epilogue|Epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[:en:Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
