[[en:Nihon Ryōiki]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Nihon Ryōiki}} [[de:Nihon Ryōiki]] [[fa:نیهون ریویکی]] [[fr:Nihon ryōiki]] [[it:Nihon ryōiki]] [[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]] [[ko:일본영이기]] [[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]] [[ru:Нихон рёики]] [[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]] [[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] {{wikidata link|Q1606838}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1606838]] Wikidata name: Nihon Ryōiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[:en:Japan|Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[:en:Translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[:en:Epilogue|Epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[:en:Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1606838]] Wikidata name: Nihon Ryōiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is [[:en:Japan|Japan]]'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[:en:Translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[:en:Epilogue|Epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[:en:Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been [[:en:Translation|translated]] into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an [[:en:Epilogue|Epilogue]]. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Raigō-in, late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ([[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|National Treasure]]) * Shinpuku-ji, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], an archaic orthography that may be used to express [[:en:Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai]]. While it is an [[:en:Early Middle Japanese|Early Middle Japanese]] text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1606838]] Wikidata name: Nihon Ryōiki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been {{ill|Translation|en|Translation|WD=Q7553|lt=translated}} into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)
{{jmyth navbox long}}

Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.

Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.

* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236

==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}

There are also a total of nine poems.

* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been {{ill|Translation|en|Translation|WD=Q7553|lt=translated}} into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been {{ill|Translation|en|Translation|WD=Q7553|lt=translated|1=Translation}} into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue|1=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236

==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|1=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese|1=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Nihon Ryōiki]][[de:Nihon Ryōiki]][[en:Nihon Ryōiki]][[fa:نیهون ریویکی]][[fr:Nihon ryōiki]][[it:Nihon ryōiki]][[ja:日本国現報善悪霊異記]][[ko:일본영이기]][[pl:Nihon-koku genpō zen-aku ryōiki]][[ru:Нихон рёики]][[uk:Ніхон Рьоікі]][[zh:日本現報善惡靈異記]]
{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been {{ill|Translation|en|Translation|WD=Q7553|lt=translated|1=Translation}} into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue|1=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|1=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese|1=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)
{{jmyth navbox long}}

There are also a total of nine poems.

* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236

Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been {{ill|Translation|en|Translation|WD=Q7553|lt=translated|1=Translation}} into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue|1=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|1=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese|1=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
== External links == * [http://miko.org/~uraki/kuon/furu/text/ryoiki/ryoiki.htm 日本靈異記] text * [https://nihonryoiki.zenwort.de/nr_htm/nr_biblio.html Bibliography] (on modern works about the NR) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071400/http://www2.dwc.doshisha.ac.jp/iwanohim/ryoikiro.html Bibliography] (Japanese works about)

{{short description|Heian period setsuwa collection}} {{italic title}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} The {{Nihongo||日本霊異記|'''Nihon Ryōiki'''}} is an early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} [[setsuwa]] collection. Written by [[Kyōkai]] between 787 and 824, it is {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}'s oldest collection of Buddhist [[setsuwa]]. It is three volumes in length.
==Title== Commonly abbreviated as ''Nihon Ryōiki'', which means "Record of Miraculous Events in Japan," the full title is {{Nihongo|''Nihonkoku Genpō Zen'aku Ryōiki''|日本国現報善悪霊異記|}}. It may also be read as ''Nihon Reiiki''. The book has been {{ill|Translation|en|Translation|WD=Q7553|lt=translated|1=Translation}} into English under the title ''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition'', but this does not represent a literal translation of the Japanese title.<ref>''Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai'', trans. and annotated with an introduction by Kyoto Motomochi Nakamura, (Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1973).</ref>
==Contents== The work is composed of three parts contained within three volumes. Each volume begins with a preface, and the final volume contains an {{ill|Epilogue|en|Epilogue|WD=Q1771692|lt=Epilogue|1=Epilogue}}. There are a total of 116 tales all dealing with Buddhist elements. There are also a total of nine poems.
==Manuscripts== There are five existing manuscripts, two of which are designated National Treasures:
* [[Kōfuku-ji]], 904 ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Raigō-in, late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ({{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)|WD=Q3245524|lt=National Treasure|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (writings: Japanese books)}}) * Shinpuku-ji, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} * Sanmai-in, 1214 * Maeda estate, 1236
All manuscripts are incomplete. The full text must be reconstructed from the multiple sources, and this was only possible after the Raigō-in manuscript was discovered in 1973.
==Linguistics== The text contains a number of words in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}, an archaic orthography that may be used to express {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|en|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|WD=Q859568|lt=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|1=Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai}}. While it is an {{ill|Early Middle Japanese|en|Early Middle Japanese|WD=Q182695|lt=Early Middle Japanese|1=Early Middle Japanese}} text, it is early enough to still preserve the distinction between ko<sub>1</sub>, ko<sub>2</sub><ref>Yoshida (2001: 147)</ref> and pe<sub>1</sub>, pe<sub>2</sub> prior to their mergers.
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References==
* {{cite book | last = Endō | first = Yoshimoto |author2=Kazuo Kasuga | title = Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1967 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-060070-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Izumoji | first = Osamu | title = Shin Iwanami Nihon Koten Bungaku Jiten: Nihon Ryōiki | publisher = [[Iwanami Shoten Publishing|Iwanami Shoten]] | year = 1996 | language = Japanese | isbn = 4-00-240030-1 }} * {{NKBJ}} * {{cite book | last = Nakamura | first = Kyoto Motomochi | title = Miraculous Stories from the Japanese Buddhist Tradition: The Nihon Ryōiki of the Monk Kyōkai | publisher = Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press | year = 1973 | isbn = 0-674-57635-7 | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/miraculousstorie0000keik }} * {{NKBD}} * {{cite book | last = Yoshida | first = Kanehiko | authorlink = |author2=Hiroshi Tsukishima |author3=Harumichi Ishizuka |author4=Masayuki Tsukimoto | title = Kuntengo Jiten | publisher = Tōkyōdō Shuppan | year = 2001 | location = [[Tōkyō]] | pages = | url = | language = Japanese | doi = | isbn = 4-490-10570-3 }}
